coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and cytocompatibility analysis of carbon nanodots derived from palmyra palm leaf for multicolor imaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Athinarayanan, Jegan
Periasamy, Vaiyapuri Subbarayan
Alatiah, Khalid A.
Alshatwi, Ali A.
coredata.dc:description: A humble and environmental benign preparation of carbon nanodots (CDs) utilizing palmyra leaves as a sustainable precursor, by hydrothermal method, is reported. The morphology, structure, optical and thermal behavior of CDs was investigated using transmission electron microscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy UV–Vis spectroscopy, X-ray diffractometer, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and thermogravimetric analysis. The fluorescent CDs have a spherical shape and size of around 5–10 nm. The TEM images suggest that crystallinity of the carbon nanodots with a lattice parameter of 0.238 nm, corresponding to the (100) diffraction facets of graphite. The synthesized CDs biocompatibility was assessed using the cell viability assay and acridine orange/ethidium bromide (AO/EB) dual staining in human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs). The cell viability assay results exhibit that CDs slightly decrease the human mesenchymal stem cells viability at high dose. However, no remarkable changes were observed in cell viability. Furthermore, we assessed the CDs influence on nuclear morphology of hMSCs. These results clearly suggest that CDs treated cells have healthy and intact nuclei. Overall, our study results revealed that CDs have excellent compatibility on cells. Thus, the synthesized CDs can be used as versatile fluorescent probe for cellular imaging because of their excitation-dependent emission. Furthermore, we utilize the CDs as fluorescent probe for cellular imaging using confocal microscope, resulting tunable fluorescent emission. The CDs can enter the cells and accumulate in cytoplasmic region, symptomatic of congenial potential for multicolor imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanoparticles
Sensing
Bio-imaging
Biomass

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CDs effect on nuclear morphology of hMSCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Digital photograph of Palmyra Palm tree and (b) its leaf powder; (c) palmyra leaf derived CDs under visible and UV light illumination; (d) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs in different concentration.
(a) Tapping mode AFM images of CDs on a mica substrate; (b) Height profiles of CDs.
(a–b) TEM images of palmyra leaves derived CDs; (c) XRD and (d) FTIR spectra of CDs.
(a) TGA and (b) DTG curves of prepared CDs.
Fluorescence spectra of prepared CDs at different excitation wavelengths.
Different concentration of CDs (control, 25, 50, 100, 200 and 400 μg/mL) effect on cell viability of human mesenchymal stem cells for 24 and 48 h.
AO/EB staining of human mesenchymal stem cells exposed to CDs (control, 100 and 200 μg/mL) for 24 and 48 h.

Bio-imaging of human mesenchymal stem cells exposed to CDs for 12 h at 50 μg/mL under confocal laser fluorescence microscope with different excitations (a) 405 nm, (b) 480 nm, (c) 568 nm and (d) overlay of 405 nm, 480 nm and 568 nm images.
Comparison of biocompatibility property of various CDs with different carbon sources.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and cytocompatibility analysis of carbon nanodots derived from palmyra palm leaf for multicolor imaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanobiotechnology and Molecular Biology Research Lab, Department of Food Science and Nutrition, College of Food and Agricultural Sciences, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2460, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanobiotechnology and Molecular Biology Research Lab
Department of Food Science and Nutrition
College of Food and Agricultural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanobiotechnology and Molecular Biology Research Lab, Department of Food Science and Nutrition, College of Food and Agricultural Sciences, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2460, Riyadh, 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Nanobiotechnology and Molecular Biology Research Lab, Department of Food Science and Nutrition, College of Food and Agricultural Sciences, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2460, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanobiotechnology and Molecular Biology Research Lab
Department of Food Science and Nutrition
College of Food and Agricultural Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A humble and environmental benign preparation of carbon nanodots (CDs) utilizing palmyra leaves as a sustainable precursor, by hydrothermal method, is reported. The morphology, structure, optical and thermal behavior of CDs was investigated using transmission electron microscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy UV–Vis spectroscopy, X-ray diffractometer, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and thermogravimetric analysis. The fluorescent CDs have a spherical shape and size of around 5–10 nm. The TEM images suggest that crystallinity of the carbon nanodots with a lattice parameter of 0.238 nm, corresponding to the (100) diffraction facets of graphite. The synthesized CDs biocompatibility was assessed using the cell viability assay and acridine orange/ethidium bromide (AO/EB) dual staining in human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs). The cell viability assay results exhibit that CDs slightly decrease the human mesenchymal stem cells viability at high dose. However, no remarkable changes were observed in cell viability. Furthermore, we assessed the CDs influence on nuclear morphology of hMSCs. These results clearly suggest that CDs treated cells have healthy and intact nuclei. Overall, our study results revealed that CDs have excellent compatibility on cells. Thus, the synthesized CDs can be used as versatile fluorescent probe for cellular imaging because of their excitation-dependent emission. Furthermore, we utilize the CDs as fluorescent probe for cellular imaging using confocal microscope, resulting tunable fluorescent emission. The CDs can enter the cells and accumulate in cytoplasmic region, symptomatic of congenial potential for multicolor imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CDs) are a new class of quasi-spherical florescent nanostructured material that has drawn considerable attention owing to their unique physicochemical behaviors, such as resistance to photobleaching, high fluorescence, easy and cost-effective preparation, highly water solubility, good cell penetrability and biocompatibility (Athinarayanan et al., 2020a; Baker and Baker, 2010; Lim et al., 2015). They have emerged as promising and non-toxic substitute to heavy metal-based semiconductor quantum dots for various applications, including sensing, catalysis, optoelectronics, bio-imaging and nanomedicine (Zhang and Yu, 2016; Yuan et al., 2016; Athinarayanan et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2015). Since the first discovery of CDs in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004), various precursors have been reported for their synthesis, varying from chemicals to natural products (Athinarayanan et al., 2019, 2020a; Wang et al., 2017). The type of precursor and the synthetic approach have a large influence on the final properties of the material. An extensive range of synthetic methods, such as bottom-up or top-down routes, have been pursued to prepare CDs. While the former includes the use of arc discharge, plasma treatment, laser ablation or electrochemical treatment on larger carbonaceous materials, in the latter CDs can be synthesized through thermal carbonization of smaller precursors during (microwave-assisted) hydrothermal or solvothermal processes (Athinarayanan et al., 2020a; Zheng et al., 2015; Roy et al., 2015). To date, a variety of CDs and CD-based based materials, with a notable engineering degree of their surface and optical properties, have been reported (Xie et al., 2012; Prato et al., 2017; Carrara et al., 2017; Arcudi et al., 2017; Dimos et al., 2017; Rizzo et al., 2018; Cadranel et al., 2018). Citric acid, sugars and amminoacids are some of the most descriptive small molecules used for the fabrication of CDs, due to their simplistic carbonization and easy availability (Arcudi et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2011; Zhai et al., 2012). However, an ever-increasing attention has been paid on simple, cost-effective and environmentally friendly methods by using derivative of renewable products or naturally occurring substances as precursors (Liu et al., 2012; Hsu et al., 2012). Moreover, an existence of several functional groups in the bio-inspired green precursors allows the preparation of self-passivated and heteroatom doped CDs, which are well-known to have a progressive impact on the final properties of the material (Xu et al., 2016). All these advantages have inspired great efforts for further development of this exciting area through the exploration of various green sources. Nevertheless, various attempts have been made to improve the emission efficiency or the synthesis yield (De and Karak, 2013; Suvarnaphaet et al., 2016).

Apart from their preparation, number of studies explored the sensing and bio-imaging ability of CDs derived from bio-inspired precursors (Hsu et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2013; Li et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2017; Teng et al., 2014). Various metal ion sensors, by using change in the CD fluorescence intensity due to energy transfer processes, have been developed. CDs with high selectivity and selectivity towards toxic metal ions such as mercury or lead, as well as ions involved in biological functions such as iron, have been reported both in aqueous solutions and in real samples (Lu et al., 2012; Guo et al., 2016; Kumar et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2014). Moreover, the nontoxic nature to cells, even at high concentration, makes them particularly valuable for biological uses. Various studies demonstrate their cell internalization and their promising use as fluorescent probes in vivo and in vitro imaging (Zhang and Yu, 2016; Yuan et al., 2016). Although remarkable progression has been made in this area over the last decade, the development of new synthetic methods for large-scale and economical production of CDs is still among the most important remaining challenges.

Borassus flabellifer (Palmyra palm) is an important tree, belongs to the Arecaceae family. It is widely occurring in the Southeast Asia and Indian subcontinent. The tree has 25–35 new fresh leaves, which are green, leathery, fan-shaped, folded, 1 – 2-m-wide and separated to the midpoint into 40–60 linear-lanceolate, marginally spiny segments (Athinarayanan et al., 2018a, 2020b). Traditionally, palmyra palm leaf has been exploited for handicrafts, roofing and manuscripts writing purposes (Athinarayanan et al., 2018a, 2018b, 2020b). However, small quantity of leaves only used appropriately remains discarded or burned in open places that leads environmental nuisance. In other hand, the palmyra palm leaf is mainly composed by cellulose, lignin and hemicellulose. Its chemical composition is highly suitable to prepare carbon based materials. Also, the palmyra palm leaf is a cheap, environmentally benign, easily available, sustainable and renewable carbon resource. To best of our knowledge, no studies have reported yet to fabricate CDs from palmyra palm leaf. Thus, we explore the use of palmyra leaves as abundant and low-cost precursors for the synthesis of CDs. Moreover, we study their biocompatibility and bio-imaging ability using in vitro approach.

Palmyra leaves were pulverized and employed as precursor for CDs synthesis. About 2 g of palmyra leaves powder was added to 100 mL of Milli-Q water and transferred in a Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was kept in the oven for 12 h at 180 °C. Consequently, the liquid portion was separated by centrifugation at 20,000 rpm for 15 min using ultracentrifuge. The obtained brown solution was filtered using 0.22 μm membrane. Finally, the CDs was dialyzed through dialysis membrane and freeze-dried for further usage.

CDs dispersed in water and drop casted on carbon-coated copper grids for TEM analyses (JEM-2100 F, Japan). The XRD characterizations were performed by using a X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku MiniFlex) operated with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). The CDs surface functional groups were investigated using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). It was operated in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 (Shimadzu FTIR, Kyoto, Japan). The thermal behavior of CDs was assessed using a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA Q500, TA instruments). The TGA curve was measured under atmospheric air and following a temperature rise at 10 °C/min up to 800 °C. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was performed on a Nanoscope V (Digital Instruments Metrology Group, model MMAFMLN) in tapping mode. The obtained image analysis were performed using WsXm 4.0 software (Nanotec Electronica S. L.), and Gwyddion 2.39. The synthesized CDs solution was excited from 310 nm to 520 nm and spectra were recorded using fluorescence spectroscopy (Jasco FP-8300 spectrofluorometer). The UV–Vis spectra of CDs were recorded on a using UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer (Varian Cary 5000 spectrophotometer) between 200 and 700 nm.

The human mesenchymal stem cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 1% penicillin/streptomycin and 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum using T25 or T75 flasks. The flasks were incubated in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C with 5% of carbon dioxide.

The prepared CDs cytocompatibility was analyzed by adopting MTT assay and AO/EB (Acridine orange/Ethidium bromide) dual staining (Athinarayanan et al., 2018c). About 10,000 cells were seeded per well in 96 well plates. After 24 h, the cells were exposed to different doses of CDs (0, 25, 50, 100, 200 and 400 μg/mL) for 24 and 48 h. After incubation, the MTT dye (20 μL) was added at the concentration of 5 mg/mL per well. Subsequently, the plates were kept in dark for 6 h. Then, the media was discarded and formazan crystals were dissolved using 200 μl of DMSO per well. The plate was scanned at 570 nm using a microplate reader. The cell viability percentage was calculated by following formula:
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
( % ) =
M e a n
O D
o f
t r e a t e d
c e l l s
M e a n
O D
of
u n t r e a t e d
c e l l s
( c o n t r o l )
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs effect on nuclear morphology of hMSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Cells were plated at the density of 5 × 105 per well in 24 well plates and incubated in a CO2 incubator. After overnight incubation, the cells were treated with CDs (0, 100 and 200 μg/mL) for 24 and 48 h. After exposure, the cells were washed with phosphate buffer saline and cells were stained using AO/EB dual stain with the concentration of 100 μg/mL. The stained cells were examined using fluorescent microscope (Carl Zeiss, Jena, Germany).

The hMSCs were grown on glass coverslips in 6 well plates. After 90% of confluence, the cells were treated with 50 μg/mL concentration of CDs for 12 h. After CDs exposure, the cells were washed with phosphate buffer saline for two times and the cells were observed under a confocal microscope (excitation wavelength: 405 nm, 480 nm, and 568 nm).

Palmyra palm is an important tree that generates huge amount of waste biomass (leaves). Interestingly, palmyra palm biomass are composed by cellulose, lignin and hemicellulose (Athinarayanan et al., 2018a, 2020b). Unfortunately, small quantity of palmyra palm leaves only utilized remains burned in open sites or thrown in open places that leads environmental nuisance. To reduce the environmental pollution, the unwanted biomass can be use in alternatively. Recent studies demonstrated that naturally available biomass have exploited to fabricate carbon materials (Zhu et al., 2013; Li et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2017; Teng et al., 2014). Thus, in this study we have utilized palmyra palm leaves as the bioprecursor for the fabrication of CDs through a hydrothermal approach. The palmyra palm tree and its leaves powder is shown in Fig. 1 a–b. The leaves are mainly composed by carbohydrates and proteins. During the hydrothermal treatment, the organic substances of the leaves are easily carbonized to CDs formed. The palmyra palm leaves were carbonized at 180 °C for 12 h. Following carbonization process, the obtained mixture was centrifuged and filtered to separate the carbon nanodots. As similar, previously a few studies reported that different kinds of biomass were utilized as sustainable precursor for fabrication of CDs (Liu et al., 2012; Hsu et al., 2012). In day light illumination, prepared CDs appears like brown color while illuminated under UV light that exhibits resilient blue emission (Fig. 1c). The results strongly agreed with earlier reports where different kind of sustainable precursors were exploited for CDs preparation (Hsu et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2013; Li et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2017; Teng et al., 2014). Typically, CDs prepared through various approaches (i.e. microwave, ultrasonication or electrochemistry) displayed a UV–Vis absorption peak at around 260–320 nm, while absorption peaks at longer wavelength are usually due to surface passivation (Athinarayanan et al., 2020a; De and Karak, 2013). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1d) displays a peak at around 275 nm because of the π → π* transition of the sp2 carbon network with a tail extending to the visible range (Athinarayanan et al., 2020a; Baker and Baker, 2010).

The AFM image in Fig. 2 a–b shows the quasi spherical shape of the as prepared CDs, with a heterogeneous size distribution between 2 and 15 nm. Additionally, the TEM images show CDs has 5–10 nm in diameter and spherical shape (Fig. 3 a). The crystal lattice observed in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 3b) indicates the crystallinity of the as prepared CDs, with a lattice parameter of 0.238 nm, consistent with the (100) diffraction facets of graphite (Baker and Baker, 2010). These results are well agreed with the AFM analysis. The XRD pattern in Fig. 3c exhibited a broad peak (002) at around 22.5°. The interlayer spacing d is 0.356 nm, in agreement with literature (Dong et al., 2014; Han et al., 2015). The increased d value of palmyra leaves derived CDs as compared to graphite (0.34 nm) suggest the presence of oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups in the former (Han et al., 2015; Peng et al., 2012). The FTIR spectrum of the prepared CDs (Fig. 3d) showed the O-H, C-H and C-N stretching vibrations at 3420 cm−1, 2920 cm-1 and 1250/1420 cm−1, respectively. The bands at 1740 cm−1, 1650 cm−1 and 1050 cm-1 are assigned to CO and C-O stretching vibrations, suggesting the CDs surface partial oxidation (Hu et al., 2017; Feng et al., 2015). The thermal behavior of CDs was assessed by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). The obtained thermogravimetric curve and its derivative form (TGA-DTG curves) are shown in Fig. 4 a and b. The weight loss of ca 8% observed at 200 °C is due to the evaporation of water. At 445 °C the weight loss was around 50% and may be ascribed to the disintegration of organic functional groups on CDs. Between 450 °C and 550 °C a constant weight loss was found. However, the weight loss of the CDs further changed between 550 °C and 800 °C, likely owing to the release of pyrogas. The TGA curve associated DTG curve shows the peak around 196 °C attributed to removal of water molecules, while the peaks at 300 °C and 460 °C are related to the thermal decomposition of surface functional groups. The emission spectrum of CDs at different excitation wavelengths is shown in Fig. 5 . The CDs emission peak is red-shifted from 380 nm to 580 nm, when increasing the excitation wavelength from 310 to 530 nm. The palmyra leaves derived CDs exhibited the excitation wavelength dependent emission is the common behavior of CDs, ascribed to the presence of different size and emissive traps of the surface of the nanoparticles (Baker and Baker, 2010; Gan et al., 2016).

Earlier studies have demonstrated that CDs are biocompatible materials and a variety of biological applications have been reported (Zhang and Yu, 2016; Yuan et al., 2016). To assess the possible use of the as prepared CDs in such type of fields, the in vitro cytotoxicity on hMSCs. As shown in Fig. 6 , the CDs do not cause any appreciable changes in the cell viability of hMSCs. The cell viability is still high when the concentration of CDs is up to 400 μg/mL concentration. As similar, earlier studies suggested that silica nanostructures has do not decreased the cell viability of human lung fibroblast cells and human mesenchymal stem cells up to 200 μg/ml, which is considered as biocompatible (Athinarayanan et al., 2015; Alshatwi et al., 2015). Table 1 shows the comparison of biocompatibility behavior of different CDs derived from various resources. As compare with previous studies, palmyra palm leaves derived CDs shows excellent biocompatibility. Also, the table indicates that CDs have size and dose-dependent cytotoxicity (Athinarayanan et al., 2019, 2020a; Arumugham et al., 2020; Arul and Sethuraman, 2018; Mewada et al., 2013). Our study cell viability assay results clearly indicate that prepared CDs are non-toxic and it may be suitable for biomedical applications.

Additionally, we have analyzed the effect of CDs on the nuclear morphology of hMSCs cells. As reported in Fig. 7 , the CDs treated and untreated cells have similar healthy and intact nuclei. Moreover, no other adverse effects were observed in nuclear morphology of hMSCs. These results are consistent with cell viability assay. As similar, previous studies demonstrated that biocompatible and non-toxic nanostructures do not alter the nuclear morphological features (Athinarayanan et al., 2017a, 2017b; Al-Hadi et al., 2017; Alshatwi et al., 2017). Thus, the prepared CDs can be utilized for biomedical applications. Due to the CDs excitation wavelength dependent emission behavior, CDs could be applied as a probe for cell or protein labelling. Thus, we have analyzed the CDs potential to label hMSCs cells. As shown in Fig. 8 , CDs easily entered in the cytoplasm and emitted blue, green and red fluorescence upon excitation at 405 nm, 480 nm, and 568 nm. Therefore, CDs are biocompatible and non-toxic materials and possess good bio-imaging ability.

In conclusion, we have reported the use of palmyra leaves as easily available, environmentally benign and cheap precursors for CDs by using a simple hydrothermal method. The TEM images confirmed that CDs have 5–10 nm in size with spherical shape. Also, the images indicate that CDs have spacing of lattice fringe is 0.238 nm related to (100) diffraction facet of graphite. The palmyra palm leaves derived fluorescent CDs showed excitation-dependent emission. The CDs do not alter the cell viability and nuclear morphological features of hMSCs up to 400 μg/mL. Overall, cellular toxicity analysis showed that CDs possess good biocompatibility to cells. The bio-imaging experiments demonstrated their good performances in cell labeling. Our study results suggested that palmyra palm leaves derived CDs could be applicable for biomedical applications.
This work was supported by the Researchers Supporting Project (RSP-2020/178), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

Jegan Athinarayanan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing - original draft, preparation. Vaiyapuri Subbarayan Periasamy: Data curation, Writing - original draft, preparation. Khalid A. Alatiah: Data curation, Writing - original draft, preparation. Ali A. Alshatwi: Visualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the Researchers Supporting Project, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia (Grant No: RSP-2020/178). We thank Professor Maurizio Prato in the Dipartimento di Scienze Farmaceutiche at the University of Trieste, Italy for his valuable suggestion and correction.
